nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated and benthopelagic; occurs along outer reef slopes and in current channels at depths of roughly 5–30 m (recorded down to 210 m). Juveniles tend to aggregate around isolated patch reefs, while adults are commonly observed forming large schools well above the bottom.

diet.notes: A natural plankton feeder. Generally accepts a varied diet of meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, and finely chopped seafood, alongside frozen or prepared planktivore formulas. Feeding several small meals per day is commonly advised given the species' high activity level and small mouth.

goodWith: other Heniochus diphreutes if introduced simultaneously

summary: A peaceful schooling butterflyfish often confused with the Moorish Idol, sporting bold black-and-white bars and a long white dorsal filament. Generally considered one of the more reef-tolerant Heniochus and tends to do best in groups with ample open swimming space.
careNotes:
Heniochus diphreutes is closely similar in appearance to Heniochus acuminatus and is sometimes nicknamed the 'False Moorish Idol' for its resemblance to Zanclus cornutus. It tends to be distinguished from H. acuminatus by a shorter snout, a more rounded body, and a more strongly schooling habit. Whereas H. acuminatus is typically observed in pairs, H. diphreutes is well-documented forming large schools, especially as adults — keepers generally report best results when keeping three or more individuals introduced together.

This species is generally considered the most reef-tolerant member of the genus, with a stronger preference for zooplankton over coral polyps. Most reefkeepers report it leaves SPS and LPS corals alone, though caution is still warranted around small ornamental shrimp, feather dusters, and clam mantles. LiveAquaria classifies it as 'reef safe with caution'.

Juveniles have been documented acting as cleaner fish at reef cleaning stations, picking parasites from larger fish — a behavior also reported, less frequently, in adults.

Temperature: LiveAquaria recommends 22.2–25.6°C (72–78°F), while FishBase wild-distribution data report a preferred range of 19.1–28.1°C (mean 26.1°C); a working target of 22–27°C averages the two. Adult size: FishBase records a maximum of 21 cm TL; LiveAquaria reports a maximum captive size of about 21.6 cm (8.5 in), so the two agree closely. Tank size: LiveAquaria recommends a minimum of 125 US gallons (~473 L) for a single specimen; given the species' strongly schooling habit (groups of three or more), active swimming style, and preference for open water, a minimum of 500 L is used here as a conservative, slightly larger nice-round value.
breedingNotes: Spawning in captivity has been very rarely documented. In the wild the species is oviparous and pairs form during the spawning season; eggs and larvae are pelagic, which makes rearing in home aquariums considered extremely difficult.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Chaetodontidae / Acanthuriformes), adult size (max 21 cm TL), depth and temperature range (preferred 19.1–28.1°C, mean 26.1°C), native range across the Indo-Pacific, planktivorous diet, and the species' strongly schooling habit in adults.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic spawner; successful captive breeding in home aquariums has rarely if ever been documented. Wild pairs spawn during the breeding season, releasing pelagic eggs.
